The check has been reverted while we re-think the fix for > `issue 265`_. >.. _issue 540: https://bitbucket.org/ned/coveragepy/issues/540/cant-install-coverage-v43-into-under >.. _issue 541: https://bitbucket.org/ned/coveragepy/issues/541/coverage-43-breaks-nosetest-with-coverage >### 4.3 >-------------------------- >- Subprocesses that are measured with `automatic subprocess measurement`_ used > to read in any pre-existing data file. This meant data would be incorrectly > carried forward from run to run. Now those files are not read, so each > subprocess only writes its own data. Fixes `issue 510`_. >- The ``coverage combine`` command will now fail if there are no data files to > combine. The combine changes in 4.2 meant that multiple combines could lose > data, leaving you with an empty .coverage data file. Fixes issues > `issue 525`_, `issue 412`_, `issue 516`_, and probably `issue 511`_. >- Coverage.py wouldn't execute `sys.excepthook`_ when an exception happened in > your program. Now it does, thanks to Andrew Hoos. Closes `issue 535`_. >.. _sys.excepthook: https://docs.python.org/3/library/sys.htmlsys.excepthook >- Branch coverage fixes: > - Branch coverage could misunderstand a finally clause on a try block that > never continued on to the following statement, as described in `issue > 493`_. This is now fixed. Thanks to Joe Doherty for the report and LoÃ¯c > Dachary for the fix. > - A while loop with a constant condition (while True) and a continue > statement would be mis-analyzed, as described in `issue 496`_. This is now > fixed, thanks to a bug report by Eli Skeggs and a fix by LoÃ¯c Dachary. > - While loops with constant conditions that were never executed could result > in a non-zero coverage report. Artem Dayneko reported this in `issue > 502`_, and LoÃ¯c Dachary provided the fix. >- The HTML report now supports a ``--skip-covered`` option like the other > reporting commands. Thanks, LoÃ¯c Dachary for the implementation, closing > `issue 433`_. >- Options can now be read from a tox.ini file, if any. Like setup.cfg, sections > are prefixed with "coverage:", so ``[run]`` options will be read from the > ``[coverage:run]`` section of tox.ini. Implements part of `issue 519`_. > Thanks, Stephen Finucane. >- Specifying both ``--source`` and ``--include`` no longer silently ignores the > include setting, instead it fails with a message. Thanks, Nathan Land and > LoÃ¯c Dachary. Closes `issue 265`_. >- The ``Coverage.combine`` method has a new parameter, ``strict=False``, to > support failing if there are no data files to combine. >- When forking subprocesses, the coverage data files would have the same random > number appended to the file name. This didn't cause problems, because the > file names had the process id also, making collisions (nearly) impossible. > But it was disconcerting. This is now fixed. >- The text report now properly sizes headers when skipping some files, fixing > `issue 524`_. Thanks, Anthony Sottile and LoÃ¯c Dachary. >- Coverage.py can now search .pex files for source, just as it can .zip and > .egg. Thanks, Peter Ebden. >- Data files are now about 15% smaller. >- Improvements in the ``[run] debug`` setting: > - The "dataio" debug setting now also logs when data files are deleted during > combining or erasing. > - A new debug option, "multiproc", for logging the behavior of > ``concurrency=multiprocessing``. > - If you used the debug options "config" and "callers" together, you'd get a > call stack printed for every line in the multi-line config output. This is > now fixed. >- Fixed an unusual bug involving multiple coding declarations affecting code > containing code in multi-line strings: `issue 529`_. >- Coverage.py will no longer be misled into thinking that a plain file is a > package when interpreting ``--source`` options. Thanks, Cosimo Lupo. >- If you try to run a non-Python file with coverage.py, you will now get a more > useful error message. `Issue 514`_. >- The default pragma regex changed slightly, but this will only matter to you > if you are deranged and use mixed-case pragmas. >- Deal properly with non-ASCII file names in an ASCII-only world, `issue 533`_. >- Programs that set Unicode configuration values could cause UnicodeErrors when > generating HTML reports. Using the examples from ipython/ipython#6896

Closes ipython/ipython#8599

When starting a kernel using the Jupyter Notebook Kernel API, web
browsers will automatically check for the presence of `x-xsrftoken` in
the Access-Control-Allow-Headers during the preflight CORS check
([ref][ref]).

[ref]: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Access-Control-Allow-Headers

Since we didn't allow this header before, web browsers would fail the
preflight check even when the x-xsrftoken header isn't being used by the
notebook server.

This meant that running a webpage on localhost:8080 that used Javascript
to start a kernel on a notebook server running on localhost:8888 would
fail.

How I tested this commit:

1. Start a notebook server using

		jupyter notebook --no-browser --NotebookApp.allow_origin="*" --NotebookApp.disable_check_xsrf=True --NotebookApp.token=''

2. Build the [web3](https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/tree/master/examples/web3) example from ipywidgets.
3. In that directory, run `npm run host`.
4. Verify that visiting http://localhost:8080/ starts a kernel in the notebook server.

Sound like you guys should just use _x.y.z_ and release plain, final releases.

pr_title: enforcing float dtype only for numeric inputs
pr_body:
To be able to cope with inputs of any type, observable arrays should inherit the dtype from the input array instead of enforcing it to be a float.

However, while simple inheritance seems to work for paramz tests, it is breaking GPy because some arrays/values are integers and underlying Cython code expects floats. Ideally I think this issue should be addressed by GPy, not paramz. This might end up leading to a lot of change in the code.

I've come with kind of "compromise": if the input is numeric it forces it to be a float. It only inherits the type in case it fails to convert the input to a float. I am not entirely happy with this solution but it seems to be enough to allow arbitrary inputs like strings, etc.

pr_title: Don't call xrandr on OS X
pr_body:
Usual OS X instalation doesn't contain the xrandr utility. One can bring
it only by installing XQuartz and even after it the utility doesn't work
well with the native windows.

Use more precise checkout (os.name == "posix" in OS X as it is
posix-compliant) to identify the Linux system and avoid redundant call.

This is a work in progress to merge databroker, metadatastore, metadataclient, filestore, portable_mds, and portable_fs into a single repository.  The plan is to put all of the code in one repository because

  - when we change API / add features all must get it in lockstep so versions should stay in sync
 - there is a lot of shared code between the various backends, this makes it easier to share code
 - simplify the number of things that need to be installed.

The zoo of dependancies will be handled at install time and with judicious top-level imports.

This will go in several phase

 - [x] merge all of the histories
 - [x] re-arrange so it installs 
 - [x] re-arrange into eventsource / header source
 - [ ] remove redundent code

pr_title: Provenance: PROV links for nested workflows
pr_body:
Tests and fixes for provenance for nested workflows and directories.

# Provenance file names for nested workflows

Renamed nested workflow provenance files to include run UUID:

```
stain@biggie:/tmp/tmp_y11y58iro/metadata/provenance$ ls
primary.cwlprov.json
primary.cwlprov.jsonld
primary.cwlprov.nt
primary.cwlprov.provn
primary.cwlprov.ttl
primary.cwlprov.xml
workflow_20compile.35c7bff2-ed72-464b-8aac-4722a57ed9e5.cwlprov.json
workflow_20compile.35c7bff2-ed72-464b-8aac-4722a57ed9e5.cwlprov.jsonld
workflow_20compile.35c7bff2-ed72-464b-8aac-4722a57ed9e5.cwlprov.nt
workflow_20compile.35c7bff2-ed72-464b-8aac-4722a57ed9e5.cwlprov.provn
workflow_20compile.35c7bff2-ed72-464b-8aac-4722a57ed9e5.cwlprov.ttl
workflow_20compile.35c7bff2-ed72-464b-8aac-4722a57ed9e5.cwlprov.xml
```

This avoids using `%` in escaping as such filenames would need to be escaped as `%25` in `manifest.json` and other PROV files.  We can argue if a human-readable prefix like `workflow_20compile` is actually needed(here derived from the step name `compile` I think?), alternateively  or we just always call it `workflow.35c7bff2-ed72-464b-8aac-4722a57ed9e5` or `nested.35c7bff2-ed72-464b-8aac-4722a57ed9e5` or just `35c7bff2-ed72-464b-8aac-4722a57ed9e5` alone?

Not yet tested, but by including the UUID the filenames should be unique per run rather than by per step name, thus should work better if there are iterations over a step, or if sub-sub-workflow have steps with the same name.


# Links to nested provenance files

Before it was hard to find the corresponding provenance file for a nested workflow. This adds two new relations, using [prov:has_provenance](https://www.w3.org/TR/prov-aq/) relation to say there is more provenance info in another file.

In `metadata/manifest.json` based directly from the wf run UUID:


```
        {
            "about": "urn:uuid:35c7bff2-ed72-464b-8aac-4722a57ed9e5",
            "content": [
                "provenance/workflow_20compile.35c7bff2-ed72-464b-8aac-4722a57ed9e5.cwlprov.xml",
                "provenance/workflow_20compile.35c7bff2-ed72-464b-8aac-4722a57ed9e5.cwlprov.provn",
                "provenance/workflow_20compile.35c7bff2-ed72-464b-8aac-4722a57ed9e5.cwlprov.json",
                "provenance/workflow_20compile.35c7bff2-ed72-464b-8aac-4722a57ed9e5.cwlprov.ttl",
                "provenance/workflow_20compile.35c7bff2-ed72-464b-8aac-4722a57ed9e5.cwlprov.nt",
                "provenance/workflow_20compile.35c7bff2-ed72-464b-8aac-4722a57ed9e5.cwlprov.jsonld"
            ],
            "oa:motivatedBy": {
                "@id": "http://www.w3.org/ns/prov#has_provenance"
            }
        }
```

and in PROV from `primary.cwlprov.*` to the `workflow-*.cwlprov.*` files:

```provn
  activity(id:35c7bff2-ed72-464b-8aac-4722a57ed9e5, -, -, [prov:type='wfprov:ProcessRun', prov:label="Run of workflow/packed.cwl#main/compile"])
  activity(id:35c7bff2-ed72-464b-8aac-4722a57ed9e5, -, -, 
     [prov:has_provenance='provenance:workflow_20compile.35c7bff2-ed72-464b-8aac-4722a57ed9e5.cwlprov.ttl', 
     prov:has_provenance='provenance:workflow_20compile.35c7bff2-ed72-464b-8aac-4722a57ed9e5.cwlprov.nt', 
     prov:has_provenance='provenance:workflow_20compile.35c7bff2-ed72-464b-8aac-4722a57ed9e5.cwlprov.xml', 
     prov:has_provenance='provenance:workflow_20compile.35c7bff2-ed72-464b-8aac-4722a57ed9e5.cwlprov.jsonld', 
     prov:has_provenance='provenance:workflow_20compile.35c7bff2-ed72-464b-8aac-4722a57ed9e5.cwlprov.json', 
     prov:has_provenance='provenance:workflow_20compile.35c7bff2-ed72-464b-8aac-4722a57ed9e5.cwlprov.provn'])
```

In both cases we see that `35c7bff2-ed72-464b-8aac-4722a57ed9e5` has more provenance in the other files. This approach should also work with other eventual provenance files from command line tools (white box provenance), even if those files are not `cwlprov` or even not even PROV - but there would need to be a way to indicate to cwltool that such files are provenance.

Untested: If there's a nested nested workflow then there would be similar has_provenance links in the intermediate PROV file, but not in the master PROV file.

(Note: As these prov files are all semantically equivalent it should not really necessary to list all of them - either with content-negotiation or `Link rel=alternate` relations to find the others. Unfortunately in RO we don't have a clear way at the moment to relate alternates so for now they are all listed directly.)


# UUID consistency

After the refactoring to split out `CreateProvProfile` class there were some attributes duplicated on `workflow_run_uuid` and so on that caused some inconsistencies in identifiers. The reason was that the old code would derive the RO UUID from the first mention of the master workflow UUID, but now the wrong attributes would be updated.

I suggest now to rather do it instead more predictable, so that the RO UUID is fixed from start, and the UUID is instead passed into the `CreateProvProfile` as a optional parameter. This also makes it easier to use the RO arcp URI earlier, such as in the new `has_provenance` links.


I've fixed so that the UUID of the RO root base URI is again equivalent to the master workflow run:

```
stain@biggie:/tmp/tmp_y11y58iro$ grep External bag-info.txt 
External-Identifier: arcp://uuid,2f2348a1-1375-4c93-8ac7-c60deff5e790/

stain@biggie:/tmp/tmp_y11y58iro$ grep :2f2348a1-1375-4c93-8ac7-c60deff5e790 metadata/provenance/primary.cwlprov.provn 
  activity(id:2f2348a1-1375-4c93-8ac7-c60deff5e790, 2018-08-09T09:33:44.444167, -, [prov:type='wfprov:WorkflowRun', prov:label="Run of workflow/packed.cwl#main"])
```

and that now the nested workflow prov files use the same arcp base URI rather than a fresh one:

```
stain@biggie:/tmp/tmp_y11y58iro$ grep arcp metadata/provenance/workflow_20compile.35c7bff2-ed72-464b-8aac-4722a57ed9e5.cwlprov.provn 
  prefix researchobject <arcp://uuid,2f2348a1-1375-4c93-8ac7-c60deff5e790/>
```

Similarly the UUID inside the nested PROV file of a nested workflow activity matches the UUID of the step in the upper PROV file (and correspond to the filename).

However the upper PROV do not currently mark the nested step as a `WorkflowRun` rather than `ProcessRun` (should it?) - to find out that you would currently have to dive into the nested PROV files or look at the plan.
 
# TODO: Wrong plan URIs

Note that the prospective/plan for nested workflows provenance still wrongly claim they are executing eleements of `packed.cwl#main`:

```
# from workflow_20compile.35c7bff2-ed72-464b-8aac-4722a57ed9e5.cwlprov.provn
  activity(id:47f527a0-0417-4e54-a0cc-5729f498747b, -, -, [prov:type='wfprov:ProcessRun', prov:label="Run of workflow/packed.cwl#main/untar"])
```

Here this should have been `packed.cwl#1st-workflow.cwl/untar` instead of using `#main`. I'll try to update this pull request to fix this.

# Directories and collections

Support for `class:Directory`, represented in PROV-N using [PROV Dictionary](https://www.w3.org/TR/prov-dictionary/):

```
  entity(id:d6ba95f7-bd67-4f2e-80c0-78840ef4b85b, [prov:type='prov:Dictionary',
           prov:type='cwlprov:Directory', prov:type='wfprov:Artifact', prov:type='prov:Collection', 
           prov:hadDictionaryMember='id:ba4ff3db-314a-4f19-b231-43d206f9fb5c', 
           prov:hadDictionaryMember='id:3a136630-30ca-4643-bec6-806d2e63b235', 
           prov:hadDictionaryMember='id:d6887434-dec2-4223-af70-6428f3af67f5'])
  hadMember(id:d6ba95f7-bd67-4f2e-80c0-78840ef4b85b, 
                     data:84a516841ba77a5b4648de2cd0dfcb30ea46dbb4)
  entity(id:d6887434-dec2-4223-af70-6428f3af67f5, 
            [prov:type='prov:KeyEntityPair', prov:pairKey="c", 
             prov:pairEntity='data:84a516841ba77a5b4648de2cd0dfcb30ea46dbb4'])
  entity(data:86f7e437faa5a7fce15d1ddcb9eaeaea377667b8, [prov:type='wfprov:Artifact'])
```

Note that currently directories themselves are identified by UUID, which could mean there could become multiple identifiers for what is effectively the same directory structure.


# Tests!

Last, but not least, I've added a bit of tests that try to verify the above, including loading up the PROV files as RDF and find the nested PROV files through the `has_provenance` link. 

I didn't use the PROV Python API for reading, as although it was able to load the PROV files it had little support for looking up. Thus some of the queries are a bit more complicated than they seem in PROVN as occassionally they have to follow n-ary relations like `qualifiedStart`.

With these identifiers sorted and tests working it should be easier to add/test more complicated PROV scenarios like nested nested workflows, filesets or scattering.  Some of the manifest/PROV testing could however with benefits be moved out to RDF Shapes to avoid deep graph traversal in Python.

Update Decimal field serialization to use fixed-point notation
pr_body:
This avoids engineering-notation output, which occurs with default Decimal type string formatting in certain situations.

While Python does not have an issue round-tripping the engineering-notation strings, I imagine the fixed-point notation [[1]](https://docs.python.org/3/library/string.html#formatspec) more compatible with other languages/parsers.  FWIW, Django REST framework also uses fixed-point notation string output for Decimals [[2]](https://github.com/tomchristie/django-rest-framework/blob/3.4.7/rest_framework/fields.py#L1018).

Old behavior:

    >>> fields.Decimal(as_string=True).serialize('x', {'x': Decimal('0.0000000')})
    '0E-7'

New behavior:

    >>> fields.Decimal(as_string=True).serialize('x', {'x': Decimal('0.0000000')})
    '0.0000000'

I updated first_solar_spectral_correction within atmosphere.py to account for changes in model that are reflected in the IEEE PVSC 43 publication. I also added data flags/filters in order to make sure that the function doesn't "blow up" when given extraordinary inputs.

Minimum precipitable water floor is set to 0.1cm. This presents the function from going toward -Inf as precipitable water goes to toward zero.

An absolute airmass ceiling is placed at AM = 10. This prevents function from destabilizing at very high AM. AM > 10 occur very close to sunset/sunrise when irradiance levels are very low.

Warning flags are now printed if Pwat is really high (above 8 cm), or if absolute air mass is very low (below .58)

pr_title: Avoid configuring DB paths via entry points in tests
pr_body:
Having the DB path configuration via an entry point allows a library user to load sqlite data without worrying about where the DB file is.
In unit tests however, it means that if we've defined an entry point to configure the path, that path will be used in the test unless we explicitly set SQLite to use an in-memory DB in the test config.
This is somehwat dangerous, since a test written by someone ignoring this could modify the actual DB that the library exports.

that produced that data has `many` set to True.

[CMake] Make MSVC flags much more similar to python/Makefile build system.
pr_body:
This PR attempts to make the compiler defines, flags and linker flags for MSVC much more similar to what the python/Makefile build system. This is motivated by problems reported in #990 . This might not fix #990 but it makes the build systems flags much similar which will help with debugging.

The most notable things are

* Removal of `compiler_flags_override.cmake`. It wasn't working correctly.
* Addition of the `msvc_legacy_quirks.cmake` file.
* Support for link time optimization. The new `LINK_TIME_OPTIMIZATION` option is equivalent
to the python/Makefile build system's `--optimize` flag for MSVC. It's also supported for Clang and GCC too but this very experimental.

This `msvc_legacy_quirks.cmake` file contains flags that I couldn't find a proper home for. I haven't duplicated some of the flags from the old build system because some of them do not make sense.

* `/D _DEBUG` is not set. [MSVC sets this itself when the runtime is set](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/b0084kay.aspx)  so this should not be set
* `/D _CONSOLE` is not set. I can't find any proper documentation for this but apparently this is [a define set in older environments](https://codeyarns.com/2010/12/02/visual-c-windows-and-console-subsystems/).
* `/D _LIB` is not set. [Apparently this macro](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/35034683/how-to-tell-if-current-project-is-dll-or-static-lib) is used indicate if building a static library. This doesn't seem to be documented though.
* `/D ASYNC_COMMANDS` is not set. I have no idea why this was being set. It is not documented and is not being used.

Other flags from the old build system are used. I've changed when some of them are used (e.g. `/D UNICODE /D _UNICODE` is set always but the old build system only set this is some configuration) and some are set but comments note that setting some don't really have a purpose.

Recently, I started to work with sparse data. Then I found lasagne, a very easy-to-use and handful module. However, it didn't support sparse input for networks. I started to make some small tweaks to `lasagne.layers.DenseLayer` and `lasagne.layers.DropoutLayer` to get it working with the `MLP` from MNIST example. Later, I found that other people might find it useful as well, [Sparse input to Lasagne #317](https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/issues/317).

A couple days ago I did a pull request of these changes. See [Sparse input to Lasagne #595] (https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/pull/595). After getting nice feedbacks, I am submitting it again for the community analysis.

Changes:
- A sparse submodule was introduced: `lasagne.layers.sparse`
- Two new classes were added, `lasagne.layers.sparse.DenseLayer` and `lasagne.layers.sparse.DropoutLayer`, that extend from `lasagne.layers.DenseLayer` and `lasagne.layers.DropoutLayer`, respectively. Both classes just overrides the `get_output_for(  )` in order to use sparse matrices instead of `theano.tensor`.

Changes attributions in code, documentation and license to include all Lasagne contributors, not only (or not primarily) Sander. He's still explicitly named as the one who started the project, though!

Copied some sections about copyright and contribution agreement from Caffe's license file. Not sure if we need to include Caffe's license file now because Caffe's license text is covered by Caffe's license text? %-)

I've chosen our google group as the email address to contact the authors under. Seemed like the most reasonable option. (Theano has its *theano-dev* group there, but we don't have nor need one. Blocks and Fuel don't give an email address at all, which would also be an option.)

This extends PR #409 with better documentation (and correct markup) and slightly simplified code. If this is merged, it closes #409.
(I originally wanted to send @Botev a PR to his PR, but since no further work is required on top, I figured it would be easier to send a new PR to Lasagne instead.)

/edit: I also swapped the meaning of alpha and beta, since I found it extremely confusing that they were not matching the order of scale_in and scale_out in the constructor parameters. For a fact, Alexander got confused by this as well and reported LeCun's suggested factors in a different order than the other ones.

pr_title: Create a unified @nteract/markdown package to use all over nteract
pr_body:
The CommonMark markdown renderer currently being used escapes certain LaTeX commands (specifically, those with a single punctuation mark following the slash: `\,`, `\;`, etc...) causing the punctuation mark to be left in. 

Switching to `react-markdown` package fixes this issue and doesn't escape anything between the MathJax delimiters (default `$` and `$$`).

Closes #2434, https://github.com/nteract/nteract/issues/2037, https://github.com/nteract/nteract/issues/2012, https://github.com/nteract/nteract/issues/1037, https://github.com/nteract/nteract/issues/1663, https://github.com/nteract/nteract/issues/1557, https://github.com/nteract/nteract/issues/1875, https://github.com/nteract/nteract/issues/2115, #1663, #1330

This also makes it so that markdown tables and strikethrough render properly. 🎉 

--------

This completely switches us over to a new package called `@nteract/markdown` to control both our mathjax and markdown rendering. This change affects:

* `@nteract/transforms` - markdown and latex transforms
* `@nteract/core` - markdown cells
* `@nteract/notebook-preview`  it sets up it's own top level `<MathJax.Context>` so there's only one for all children
* `nteract` desktop - for now, until nteract/mathjax-electron#30 is resolved, we're relying on MathJax v2.7.1 from cdnjs.
* `@nteract/commuter` - rendering of markdown documents, zeppelin notebooks, and all jupyter notebook rendering

By default the markdown renderer and the latex output will create their own `<MathJax.Context>` if there's not already one. For any apps where you want to control the MathJax setup, wrap the highest level component that should have context (for a notebook, the cells).

```
<MathJax.Context>
  <App />
</MathJax.Context>
```

These are the props for `<MathJax.Context>`:

```js
Props = {
  children: React.Node,
  didFinishTypeset: ?() => void,
  script: string | false,
  input: "ascii" | "tex",
  delay: number,
  options: Object,
  loading: React.Node,
  noGate: boolean,
  onError: (err: Error) => void,
  onLoad: ?Function
};
```

pr_title: Implements target_system lookup. Implements better target semantics.
pr_body:
Exposing a `target_system` on `connect()` is probably not what we want. Exposing `source_system` might be, though.

This does a bit of an overhaul over the MAVLink object in the backend to make sure we get this right. The `target_system` is loaded from the first heartbeat we get and fixed after that point. We also have some backend classes which we might expose eventually, but for now demonstrate the use of multiple target systems in one program. e.g.

```
dronekit-sitl copter
```

In one window,

```
from dronekit import connect
from dronekit.mavlink import MAVConnection

v = connect('tcp:127.0.0.1:5760', wait_ready=True, source_system=255)

o = MAVConnection('udpout:127.0.0.1:14550', source_system=254)

v._handler.pipe(o)
o.start()

import time
while True:
	time.sleep(1)
```

in the next, and 

```
mavproxy.py --master 0.0.0.0:14550 --source-system=254
```

Works to daisy chain a connection _through_ DroneKit.

This fixes some issues in LHCbDataset. The ```difference```, ```symmetricDifference``` and ```union``` functions were calling ```extend()``` with a ```[list(set)]```. So the set was being changed to a list but then put inside another list which was causing problems. I don't know if the extra square brackets were there for a reason.

This fixes #125 by basically removing the whole mechanism and just sending the 'files.py' file and referencing it appropriately. This **may** bite us in the future as we can no longer 'easily' send Ganga modules with jobs. However, users can't do this anyway and we've gone this far without needing to send any more so I think it's worth the risk :)

This adds a module to `Ganga.Utility` to provide the dumping of thread stacktraces to a file. It can be used with:
```python
from Ganga.Utility import stacktracer
stacktracer.trace_start('trace.html')
```
That code that can, for example, be added to a user's `~/.ganga.py` or just run live.

It may be useful in the future to incorporate this with `report()` or maybe the monitoring thread heartbeat monitor. I think we should also think about running this alongside the test suite so that locked-up tests can be debugged even on Jenkins.

This small PR fixes an issue the Atlas release managers were having when trying to create a `.gangarc` after switching off AFS. The problem was two fold:

1. The default GLITE_SETUP script points to an AFS version which would obviously fail here
2. If a proxy isn't created, then it fails to start up the Atlas plugin

This PR tackles both of these issues.

I'd prefer to get this out ASAP - any objections for pushing 6.6.1 for the end of the week?

pr_title: add FutureWarning about swt2 coefficient order
pr_body:
This adds a `FutureWarning` that the order of the list returned by `swt2` (and accepted as input by `iswt2`) will be swapped in future release.  Doing this was discussed in #81. pr_title: Readability improvements for examples, bugfix & improvement in patches.py
pr_body:
I made a couple of changes for the examples as well as the patches.py file. 

### patches.py
- Removed unused `namedtuple` import
- Made the `end` of the `Patch` constructor _Optional_, since the newest patch has no `end` (yet)
- `Patch.from_str` now searches through patch versions instead of patch names. Previously, searching for `Season x` would always return the first Patch in that season instead of a specific patch (e.g. `'7.12'`)
- Changed the `patches` array from passing `datetime.datetime` objects to the  `start` and `end` parameters of `Patch` (which doesn't make too much sense, considering patches have only a release *date*) to passing a `datetime.date`.
- Added patch 7.12 and 7.13.

### examples
- Most notable is the change of function names from `test_cass` to names that better reflect what the functions actually do.
- Some minor cosmetic changes, for example putting a space between a comment and the first letter in it
- Removed redundant `return` statements at the end of the functions, since Python implicitly returns `None` anyways
- Added a string denoting what's being printed out to examples where it was applicable
- Removed some unnecessary imports and assignments. For example, the `mastery.py` example would import `Summoner` and assign `name = "Kalturi"` although both weren't needed in the example.
- Moved name for Summoners in some examples to function parameters. 

If I should change anything, please let me know. 😄

pr_title: Streams stdout/stderr to logger using threads
pr_body:
subprocess.communicate() blocks and reads all the output into
memory before returning. It is safe in that it uses threads to
avoid deadlocks when capturing both stdout and stderr in a pipe.

This patch uses threads to read the stdout and stderr pipes, and
to log the output as it is generated. This approach continues to
avoid deadlocks, but also avoids blocking and reading all the output
into memory.

pr_title: Fix commissioning problem due to retransmissions
pr_body:
We had a commissioning problem on a nrf52 chip.

nrf52 802.15.4 driver is running from IRQ context and it uses the rx queue. That's why it queues all retransmitted messages during commissioning procedure. Probably you didn't notice this problem using TI, because it's driver does not have rx queues and it drops DTLS retransmissions during commissioning procedure.

We didn't implement crypto hardware acceleration in nrf52 driver yet and some procedures take more than 8 seconds.

Because of that typical commissioning procedure looks like following diagram:
`
    Joiner                        Commissioner
(1) |      Client Hello           |
    |---------------------------->|
    |                             |
(2) |      Hello Verify Request   |
    |<----------------------------|
    |                             |
(3) |      Client Hello           |
    |---------------------------->|
8 s |                             |
(4) |      Client Hello           |  retransmission
    |---------------------------->|
    |                             |
(5) |      Server Hello           |
    |      Server Key Exchange    |
    |      Server Hello Done      |
    |<----------------------------|
    |                             |
(6) |      Server Hello           |  response to retransmission
    |      Server Key Exchange    |
    |      Server Hello Done      |
    |<----------------------------|
8 s |                             |
(7) |      Server Hello           |  retransmission
    |      Server Key Exchange    |
    |      Server Hello Done      |
    |<----------------------------|
    |                             |
(8) |      Client Key Exchange    |
    |      Change Cipher Spec     |
    | Encrypted Handshake Message |
    |---------------------------->|
    |                             |
(9) |      Change Cipher Spec     |
    | Encrypted Handshake Message |
    |      Application Data       |
    |<----------------------------|
    |                             |
(10)|      Client Key Exchange    |  retransmission
    |      Change Cipher Spec     |
    | Encrypted Handshake Message |
    |---------------------------->|
    `

We found 2 problems:
1. Joiner queues retransmissions (6) and (7) during reply (8) preparation. After message flight (8) is sent Joiner receives queued retransmissions, but it removes only 1 record from it's queue (instead of all 6 records: (6) - Server Hello, (6) - Server Key Exchange, (6) - Server Hello Done, (7) - Server Hello, (7) - Server Key Exchange, (7) - Server Hello Done). When Joiner receives (9) it cannot parse it due to old records in the queue.
2. Commissioner does not retransmit message flight (9) when it receives retransmission (10). According to RFC 6347: 4.2.4 it should retransmit to avoid dead lock.

This PR solves both problems. And it makes commissioning procedure robust.

the default-constructed value with needing a reset. This fixes two
issues:

would construct an instance of the optional type and then move-assign
it, which fails if the value type isn't move-assignable.

2. With older versions of Boost, the `= {}` could fail because it is
ambiguous, allowing construction of either `boost::none` or the value
type.

This reverts commit 15f1405fff7083bf5d4f599a58c54a43be499740.

This was a clean revert with no conflicts.

The reason we're reverting this is in some versions of openssl, we're hitting issues where the challenge cert is used even if the CA requests the wrong protocol through ALPN. This is on top of the issues we were already aware of with using the challenge cert if ALPN isn't used. Since we can't provide a correct implementation and its only purpose is to help boulder with their testing, let's rip it out and have boulder implement the code for their tests.

Issue #3672

Hi,

I've added the option `normalize_username_function`. My use case is as follows:
* users should be able to use case-insensitive usernames
* the username passed on to LDAP and Jupyterhub should be lowercase.

The new option is implemented as a list. The first element should be a callable, other elements are ignored. The callable must take one argument (the supplied username) and return a 'normalized' username. Username normalization happens right before `valid_username_regex` check so the normalization has to pass this check.

Example:
`LDAPAuthenticator.normalize_username_function = [str.lower]`

The reason for using a list is that my traitlets version doesn't support a callable type. Is there a better way to enforce the option to be of type "callable"?

Are there any security to concerns with this approach?

Happy to receive feedback.

pr_title: API for plugins to request input from the user
pr_body:
I have taken a bash at creating an API for plugins to request input from a user. I have only implemented in solution for the standard `streamlink_cli`, but I have tried to design it in a way that other front-ends can use. 

During the `Plugin.bind` call and additional (optional) parameter is passed - an instance of `UserInputRequester`, the job of which is to provide a way for the user to give an input. Implementations of `UserInputRequester` must implement two methods, `ask` and `ask_password`. `ask` is for users to input non-sensitive data, eg. username. `ask_password` is for users to input sensitive data, eg. password. For example, in the `ConsoleUserInputRequester` the two methods (`ask`, and `ask_password`) are handled by `Console.ask` and `Console.askpass`. 

For a `Plugin` to request input from the user, there are two new methods `Plugin.input_ask` and `Plugin.input_ask_password` that call `UserInputRequester.ask` and `UserInputRequester.ask_password` respectively. If no `UserInputRequester` is given and a `Plugin` tries to request user input, it will raise a `PluginError`. 

As a user of the `Streamlink` API, the `UserInputRequester` instance can set by setting the option `user-input-requester`, using `Sreamlink` options constructor parameter. It must be set when instance of `Streamlink` is created to affect built-in plugins.

I mainly created this as some way is needed for the `SteamBroadcast` plugin (#1717) to request input from the user for 2FA, and we need to maintain the disconnect between `streamlink` and `streamlink_cli`.

I've tested with `python2.7`, after `authenticate_user{}` for User that not requires password change on auth.
Please, review and comment.

pr_title: Steady state emissions from concentrations to.l
pr_body:
This goes some way to helping with #28 as it provides a simple way to calculate natural emissions of CH4 and N2O for varying lifetime runs.

Example for a pulse emission of methane:

```
import numpy as np
from fair.forward import fair_scm
from fair.constants import lifetime
from fair.tools import steady

# get steady state natural emissions for a 4-year methane lifetime and 
# preindustrial concentration of 722ppb
steady_ch4_4yr = steady.emissions(species="CH4", lifetime=4, C=722.)
steady_n2o     = steady.emissions(species="N2O", C=275.)

# load up default lifetimes but override methane to 4 years
lt = lifetime.aslist
lt[1] = 4.0

# 10 Gt methane pulse
emissions = np.zeros((100, 40))
emissions[0,3] = 10000.

# turn off forcing for everything except methane
scale = np.zeros(13)
scale[1] = 1.0
C_pi = np.zeros((31))
C_pi[0:3] = [278., 722., 275.]

# run FAIR
C,F,T = fair_scm(emissions, lifetimes=lt, natural = [steady_ch4_4yr, steady_n2o], F_volcanic=0., F_solar=0., scale=scale, fixPre1850RCP=False)
```

pr_title: Allowing config to have humans after bots. Fixes #175.
pr_body:
Human index is supposed to be 0 for the first human, 1 for the second human, etc, without counting bots. Player index includes bots, therefore passing it directly into the human index field was incorrect.

I made a little class to help us keep track of the human index across multiple calls to load_bot_config.

This PR also includes an updated core dll with the following fixes:
- We were attempting to sort player configs so that humans spawned first, but the sort comparator had a serious bug that stopped it from working properly
- Once I fixed the sorting, I found that the mechanism to map the original player index to the sorted game objects was never fully implemented. Hidden by the sorting bug.
- Once I fixed the mapping, I found that our game data reader never utilized the mapping, so I fixed that.

After this fix, you can now configure a human or party_member_bot at any index you like.

pr_title: treat None values as empty string (instead of 'None')
pr_body:
in our application the backend (sqlalchemy) returns None values, which by default are rendered as 'None' strings in the form. We had to manually convert them to empty strings when (de-) serializing. instead of either a) creating our own String type or b) manually converting them in all our views, we tried adding this treatment directly to colander.

the question is: does serializing the None value to the string 'None' ever make sense in the context colander is used? (it makes perfect sense for pythons `unicode(None)` to return `'None'`, of course).

what do you think? does the change we made make sense?

I hope I did not miss any code that relied on this but it worked for me quite good and is django1.3 compatible.  I did not touch the mongodb compatibility code as this has no visible timezone support for me.
